title: Agent, goal and utility models

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Agent, goal and utility models; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; AI Knowledge Area: fundamental issues, intelligent agents, history, and societal context; atomic topic: Agent, goal and utility models
sources.claim: An agent receives percepts, maintains any needed state, chooses actions, and is evaluated against an explicit performance measure or utility under bounded information and resources.

canonicalExplanation.summary: An agent receives percepts, maintains any needed state, chooses actions, and is evaluated against an explicit performance measure or utility under bounded information and resources.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A cleaning agent observes location and dirt state, can move or clean, and is scored on cleanliness, energy, collisions, and time rather than only number of actions. Your goal is to specify a simple agent's percepts, actions, objective and performance measure. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf problem io; python quality project; Problem and environment characteristics. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: An agent receives percepts, maintains any needed state, chooses actions, and is evaluated against an explicit performance measure or utility under bounded information and resources. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: A goal does not specify every trade-off, a utility encodes value choices, rationality is relative to information and constraints, and optimizing a proxy can exploit gaps in the intended objective. The learner must reject this overclaim: A rational agent always finds the objectively best action, and a numeric reward is a neutral statement of the real goal.

includes: Relate perception, state, actions, goals, utility and rationality under bounded information.
Specify a simple agent's percepts, actions, objective and performance measure.
excludes: A goal does not specify every trade-off, a utility encodes value choices, rationality is relative to information and constraints, and optimizing a proxy can exploit gaps in the intended objective.

outcomes: Specify a simple agent's percepts, actions, objective and performance measure.

examples.title: Agent, goal and utility models: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A cleaning agent observes location and dirt state, can move or clean, and is scored on cleanliness, energy, collisions, and time rather than only number of actions.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A cleaning agent observes location and dirt state, can move or clean, and is scored on cleanliness, energy, collisions, and time rather than only number of actions.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Specify percepts, state, actions, constraints, stakeholders, and a bounded performance measure; treat utility and reward as contestable proxy choices.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Specify percepts, state, actions, constraints, stakeholders, and a bounded performance measure; treat utility and reward as contestable proxy choices.

examples.title: Agent, goal and utility models: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: An agent rewarded only for items collected blocks other users to capture every item, revealing that the reward omitted cooperation and safety constraints.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: An agent rewarded only for items collected blocks other users to capture every item, revealing that the reward omitted cooperation and safety constraints.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: A goal does not specify every trade-off, a utility encodes value choices, rationality is relative to information and constraints, and optimizing a proxy can exploit gaps in the intended objective.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: A rational agent always finds the objectively best action, and a numeric reward is a neutral statement of the real goal.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: A rational agent always finds the objectively best action, and a numeric reward is a neutral statement of the real goal.

analogy.example: a map that labels what an automated system observes, claims, chooses, and cannot establish can introduce Agent, goal and utility models only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.

limitations: maps simplify contested intelligence concepts and cannot turn benchmark evidence into general capability

recap.summary: You should now be able to specify a simple agent's percepts, actions, objective and performance measure. Keep this boundary in view: A goal does not specify every trade-off, a utility encodes value choices, rationality is relative to information and constraints, and optimizing a proxy can exploit gaps in the intended objective.
